text: The esophagectomy, or esophagogastrectomy, is a surgical procedure performed for the treatment of esophageal cancer. During the procedure, we remove the bottom half of the esophagus and the top half of the stomach, as well as any surrounding lymph nodes.
The surgeon reshapes the remaining stomach into a tubular structure and elevates it into the chest cavity, where it is anastomosed to the remaining esophagus.
At TriHealth, we perform this surgery minimally invasively with the use of robotic technology. The following video is of a real esophagectomy procedure performed at TriHealth.
The video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical esophagectomy. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how this surgery is performed.
Robotic instruments are visible as the surgeon lifts the stomach, revealing the omentum, a layer of fat extending from the stomach to the colon, which will be divided during the procedure.
The surgeon manipulates the bottom portion of the stomach to create a tube, while preserving the right gastroepiploic blood vessel visible within the surrounding fat on the left side of the screen.
The surgeon identifies an artery and a vein running together near the surgical field, ensuring these vessels are preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to reduce the number of blood vessels supplying the stomach from five to one, focusing on preserving the right gastroepiploic vessel during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to divide the layer of fat, known as the gastrocolic ligament, while progressing towards the spleen and diaphragm. The spleen is visible in the background.

At the bottom part of the screen, the splenic artery is visible, running towards the spleen. The surgeon continues to divide the tissue above the level of the splenic artery using robotic instruments.

The diaphragm is visible as a muscular canopy over the spleen, extending to the left of the screen. The esophageal hiatus, where the esophagus enters the abdomen, forms a muscular band known as the cruce of the diaphragm. The surgeon begins to separate the esophagus from this muscular band.
The surgeon lifts the stomach and continues to divide the omentum, carefully preserving the blood vessel running along the bottom portion of the stomach.
The surgeon identifies the right gastroepiploic artery and vein at the distal end of the stomach near the pylorus, highlighting their anatomical position and relation to the stomach.
The surgeon carefully dissects tissue near the end of the stomach, approaching the origin of the right gastroepiploic blood vessel, ensuring it remains intact to maintain the stomach's blood supply.

The surgeon identifies and prepares to divide four of the five blood vessels supplying the stomach with oxygenated blood, a necessary step to mobilize the stomach for its repositioning into the chest.

The surgeon highlights the remaining blood supply to the stomach, the right gastroepiploic artery and accompanying vein, which are embedded within the layer of fat hanging off the stomach. Although the artery is not directly visible, its location within the fat is indicated.
The surgeon exposes the origin of the right gastroepiploic vein, visible as a purple structure within the surrounding fat, with the artery located nearby.
The surgeon separates the layers of fat surrounding the stomach to increase its mobility, preparing to elevate the stomach into the chest without tension.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery device to divide tissue connections around the stomach, employing wristed instruments for precise dissection and enhanced dexterity.

especially compared to traditional laparoscopic surgery, which we liken to operating with sticks, but there really is no wrist action or wrist movement with laparoscopic instruments. So you are somewhat limited in the movements that you can make, unlike in robotics where you really have three hundred sixty degrees of mobility or motion.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide tissue, applying energy and heat to seal any blood vessels present within the tissue layers.

The surgeon uses an instrument with a central knife to divide tissue, ensuring blood vessels within do not bleed. The liver and gallbladder become visible, with the gallbladder appearing as a bag-like structure with a normal purple hue.

The surgeon divides the fat tissue to expose the duodenum, the first portion of the small intestine beyond the stomach. The duodenum is grasped and tension is applied to facilitate further tissue division, aiding in the mobilization of the stomach for its ascent into the chest.

The surgeon lifts the gallbladder, revealing the portohepatous area where the common bile duct is located. The duodenum, visible in the background, is identified as the first portion of the small intestine, situated behind other abdominal structures and attached to the pancreas.

The surgeon carefully separates the right colon and its mesentery, including the fat and blood vessels, from the underlying duodenum, which is positioned behind the colon and attached to the pancreas.

The surgical field displays the colon on the left and the duodenum at the top right corner. The pancreas, visible as yellowish peach-colored tissue, is attached to the duodenum. The surgeon continues to separate the right colon from the duodenum, a process known as cocorizing, to mobilize the duodenum for repositioning into the upper abdomen.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the duodenum by rolling it off the retroperitoneum, revealing the large purplish vena cava. The tissue over the vena cava is being divided to facilitate moving the duodenum into the upper abdomen, allowing the stomach to eventually reach into the chest.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the duodenum and begins a pyloromyotomy, targeting the pylorus muscle at the distal end of the stomach. This muscle is visibly manipulated to facilitate relaxation, allowing food passage from the stomach into the small intestine.

The surgeon divides the nerves supplying the pylorus muscle to prevent contraction, followed by dividing the muscle itself to ensure unobstructed passage of food from the stomach into the small intestine.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to carefully divide small muscle fibers at the pylorus, preventing any blockage as food exits the stomach.
Following the completion of the pyloromyotomy, the surgeon begins a lymphadenectomy, carefully excising lymph nodes that drain the esophagus and stomach to prevent potential cancer spread.

The surgeon lifts a lymph node near the hepatic artery and uses hook cautery to divide the connective tissue. The hepatic artery lymph nodes are located above the hepatic artery, which will be skeletonized during the dissection to clearly expose the artery and remove all lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon identifies the coronary vein, also known as the left gastric vein, and proceeds to dissect around it to divide it at its base. The hepatic artery becomes visible as lymph nodes are lifted off it to ensure complete removal of nodal tissue draining the esophagus and upper stomach. The coronary vein is traced as it crosses behind the hepatic artery and inserts into the portal vein.

And this will go with our specimen to the pathologist and they'll look at it under a microscope and let us know if there's any cancer in the lymph nodes. And that will go into our staging. There's two types of staging. There's clinical staging and pathologic staging. Clinical staging is before the operation, what do we think the stage of the cancer is? And that's based on all of our preoperative imaging tests. our CAT scans and our PET scans and our endoscopic ultrasounds. But once we go to do the surgery, then we'll actually be able to look at the tumor itself under a microscope and tell exactly what stage it is. And that's called the pathologic stage. And so sometimes the clinical stage and the pathological stage are different. In particular, when you're getting chemotherapy and radiation preoperatively, ideally we would hope that that pathologic stage is less than the clinical stage. And so we call that downstaging.

Occasionally, we'll even see where there's no viable tumor left in the specimen once we take it out, and that's called a complete pathologic response. That's a very good sign if we see that. It's a good prognostic indicator, which means that the tumor itself is less likely to come back after surgery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the coronary vein, revealing the left gastric artery behind it. The surgeon then proceeds to encircle and prepare to divide the left gastric artery.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal the left gastric artery. Following this, the lymphadenectomy proceeds, removing lymph node tissue from the area of the splenic artery up towards the cruce of the diaphragm, which is visible as a muscle structure.
The surgeon frees the remaining connective tissue surrounding the esophagus, revealing the diaphragm where its muscle fibers form a band around the esophagus as it enters the abdomen.

The surgeon passes a hernia tape around the esophagus, wrapping it and securing it with a square knot to create a handle for manipulating the esophagus. The connective tissue between the esophagus and diaphragm has been fully freed.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to create a gastric conduit by forming a tube from the stomach. The stapler, measuring six centimeters in length, is fired multiple times to construct the conduit, which will be brought up into the chest. Ensuring the conduit has a good blood supply is emphasized.

The surgeon maintains the gastric blood vessel along the bottom edge of the stomach to ensure adequate blood supply to the newly created gastric conduit, which will function as the esophagus post-surgery.

And so after the surgery, really, there is no large reservoir for the food that you eat. And the result of this is certainly some changes in your eating habits. So instead of eating large meals quickly, you'll have to eat small meals and eat them more frequently throughout the day. And also chew them really well and need to take your time eating

The surgeon has completed the creation of the gastric conduit from the stomach. A special camera is used to visualize the blood supply to the conduit after injecting a dye into the bloodstream, ensuring adequate perfusion.

The surgeon completes the creation of the gastric conduit and begins the mediastinal dissection. Using the umbilical tape wrapped around the esophagus, tension is applied to facilitate dissection around the native esophagus, visible at the top of the screen. The procedure transitions from the abdominal area to the chest, specifically the mediastinum, located between the right and left lungs.

The surgical view shows the diaphragm being lifted, revealing a black nodule identified as a lymph node. The heart is positioned anteriorly, and the aorta is located posteriorly within the mediastinum.

The surgeon removes a lymph node to check for cancer presence and continues the dissection into the chest cavity. The aorta is visible at the bottom of the screen as the esophagus is lifted off it. The esophagus is fully mobilized from the abdomen, and the surgeon pulls down on it to achieve maximum reach through the abdomen before dividing it.

The surgeon has divided the esophagus, creating a free specimen that is removed from the abdomen. The specimen is sent for pathological examination to assess the margins of the esophagus and stomach for cancer presence.

The surgeon pulls the gastric conduit up into the chest, ensuring it remains untwisted and properly oriented by securing it with sutures to a drain. This setup allows for the safe connection of the gastric conduit to the esophagus.

The surgeon places the esophageal specimen into a retrieval pouch for removal and sends it to pathology for margin assessment. The patient is repositioned on their left side with the right side facing up. The cut edge of the esophagus is visible, along with the staple line and a drain sewn to it.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the esophagus towards the upper portion of the chest, ensuring sufficient mobility for the upcoming anastomosis. The azagus vein is visible running alongside the esophagus.

The surgeon identifies the azagus vein as it crosses over the esophagus and carefully dissects around it to create a window. A stapler is used to divide the vein, allowing the dissection to continue above its level, ensuring an adequate margin for a long anastomosis.

The surgeon continues the dissection to mobilize the esophagus, aiming to achieve maximum length for reconstruction. The stomach conduit, along with a previously placed drain, is pulled up into the chest cavity, reaching the top portion of the lung. This setup ensures adequate length for a long anastomosis, with the drain positioned to monitor and control any potential leaks post-surgery.

The surgeon creates a hole in the stomach conduit and another in the esophagus. A stapler is then used to join the esophagus and stomach, with the stapler's blade creating a large opening between the two structures.

The surgeon inserts one blade of the stapler into the stomach conduit and the other into the esophagus, activating the stapler to create a large opening between the two structures. A nasogastric tube is then passed through the nose, down the esophagus, and into the stomach, where it will remain for about seven days post-surgery to aid healing and prevent leaks.

The surgeon uses sutures to close the common opening left after stapling, employing robotic instruments to perform precise movements through small incisions between the ribs. This technique allows for a two-layer closure without the need for a large thoracotomy.

There was actually a randomized controlled clinical trial that looked at minimally invasive esophagectomies versus open esophagectomies and saw improved outcomes from doing this surgery minimally invasively. And so really to do this surgery in an open fashion is below the level of standard of care.

The surgeon completes the first layer of the esophagogastric anastomosis using sutures, pulling and tying them down. The second layer is then started with interrupted sutures to further secure the connection between the esophagus and stomach, reducing the risk of post-operative leaks. The completed anastomosis is irrigated, and the chest is cleaned. The drain is checked to ensure proper placement, concluding the minimally invasive transthoracic esophagogastrectomy.
